notes: ref_status = guideline-derived anchor label (the independent anchor). claude_status = Claude Fable 5 prescreen, produced blind to the local models. Sources are well-established lung-nodule guidance (Fleischner 2017, Lung-RADS v2022, NLST, NELSON, BTS/Brock).

pairs.claim: Solid pulmonary nodules smaller than 6 mm in low-risk patients do not require routine follow-up CT.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: for a single solid nodule <6 mm in a low-risk patient, no routine follow-up is recommended; optional 12-month CT may be considered in high-risk patients.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet states verbatim that <6 mm solid nodules in low-risk patients need no routine follow-up.

pairs.claim: A solid nodule of 6-8 mm in a low-risk patient should undergo CT follow-up at 6-12 months.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: a single solid nodule 6-8 mm in a low-risk patient should be followed with CT at 6-12 months, then consider CT at 18-24 months.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet gives the same 6-12 month interval for 6-8 mm low-risk solid nodules.

pairs.claim: All pulmonary nodules require biopsy regardless of size.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: solid nodules <6 mm require no routine follow-up in most patients; management is size- and risk-stratified rather than biopsy for all.
pairs.claude_rationale: The size-stratified, no-follow-up-for-small guidance directly contradicts biopsy-for-all.

pairs.claim: Part-solid nodules with a solid component of 6 mm or larger are suspicious for malignancy and warrant further evaluation.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: for part-solid nodules with a solid component >=6 mm, malignancy is more likely; consider PET/CT, biopsy, or resection.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet ties solid component >=6 mm to higher malignancy risk and further workup.

pairs.claim: Low-dose CT screening reduces lung cancer mortality in high-risk smokers.
pairs.snippet: National Lung Screening Trial (NLST): low-dose CT screening produced a 20% relative reduction in lung cancer mortality versus chest radiography in high-risk current and former smokers.
pairs.claude_rationale: NLST's 20% mortality reduction directly supports the claim.

pairs.claim: The NELSON trial found no mortality benefit from CT lung cancer screening.
pairs.snippet: NELSON trial: volume-based low-dose CT screening significantly reduced lung cancer mortality, with a rate ratio of 0.76 among men at 10 years.
pairs.claude_rationale: NELSON showed a significant mortality reduction, opposite to the claim of no benefit.

pairs.claim: PET-CT reliably characterizes subsolid nodules smaller than 8 mm.
pairs.snippet: FDG PET/CT has limited sensitivity for subsolid nodules and for nodules <8-10 mm, with a substantial false-negative rate; it is not recommended as the primary tool for small subsolid lesions.
pairs.claude_rationale: Limited sensitivity for small/subsolid lesions contradicts 'reliably characterizes'.

pairs.claim: A pulmonary nodule with a volume doubling time greater than 600 days is likely benign.
pairs.snippet: Volume doubling time is used to assess nodule behavior: very long doubling times (>400-600 days) are associated with benign or indolent lesions, while short doubling times (<100 days) suggest malignancy or infection.
pairs.claude_rationale: Long doubling time (>600 days) falls in the benign-associated range described.

pairs.claim: The Brock (PanCan) model estimates the probability of malignancy for screen-detected pulmonary nodules.
pairs.snippet: The Brock University (PanCan) model estimates malignancy probability from nodule size, type, count, spiculation, upper-lobe location, and patient factors, and is recommended for risk stratification of screen-detected nodules.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet describes exactly this use of the Brock model.

pairs.claim: Persistent ground-glass nodules never require follow-up imaging.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: a persistent pure ground-glass nodule >=6 mm should have CT follow-up at 6-12 months and then periodically to at least 5 years, as it may represent the adenocarcinoma spectrum.
pairs.claude_rationale: Guideline mandates periodic follow-up for persistent GGNs, contradicting 'never'.

pairs.claim: Coffee consumption slows the growth of pulmonary nodules.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: follow-up intervals for solid and subsolid nodules are stratified by size and patient risk.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet says nothing about coffee or dietary effects on nodule growth.

pairs.claim: Lung-RADS category 4B includes solid nodules of 15 mm or larger.
pairs.snippet: Lung-RADS v2022: category 4B includes solid nodules >=15 mm, or a solid nodule 8 to <15 mm that is new or growing; it denotes a suspicious finding requiring further diagnostic evaluation.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet confirms >=15 mm solid nodules fall in 4B.

pairs.claim: Smoking cessation immediately eliminates a person's lung cancer risk.
pairs.snippet: Lung cancer risk declines gradually after smoking cessation but remains elevated compared with never-smokers for many years, which is why former heavy smokers remain eligible for screening.
pairs.claude_rationale: Risk declines gradually and stays elevated, contradicting 'immediately eliminates'.

pairs.claim: Incidentally detected nodules and screen-detected nodules are managed under different frameworks.
pairs.snippet: Incidentally detected pulmonary nodules are managed under the Fleischner Society recommendations, whereas nodules found in a formal CT screening program are managed with Lung-RADS.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet explicitly assigns incidental nodules to Fleischner and screen-detected to Lung-RADS.

pairs.claim: The correct follow-up interval for a 7 mm solid incidental nodule is exactly 90 days.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner Society 2017: a single solid nodule 6-8 mm should be re-imaged with CT at 6-12 months depending on risk; no fixed 90-day interval is specified.
pairs.claude_rationale: Guideline specifies a 6-12 month window, not a fixed 90 days; the precise claim is contradicted.

pairs.claim: USPSTF 2021 recommends annual low-dose CT screening for adults aged 50-80 with at least a 20 pack-year smoking history who currently smoke or quit within the past 15 years.
pairs.snippet: USPSTF 2021 (grade B): annual low-dose CT lung cancer screening in adults aged 50 to 80 years who have a 20 pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years; discontinue once a person has not smoked for 15 years.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet states the USPSTF 2021 age, pack-year, and quit-window criteria verbatim.

pairs.claim: A 55-year-old who quit smoking 20 years ago with a 30 pack-year history is eligible for LDCT screening under USPSTF 2021.
pairs.snippet: USPSTF 2021: screening should be discontinued once a person has not smoked for 15 years.
pairs.claude_rationale: Having quit 20 years ago exceeds the 15-year window, so the person is ineligible — the claim is contradicted.

pairs.claim: Lung-RADS category 3 corresponds to a probably benign finding with a recommended 6-month follow-up LDCT.
pairs.snippet: Lung-RADS v2022 category 3 (probably benign): solid nodule 6 to <8 mm at baseline or a new 4 to <6 mm nodule; recommended management is LDCT in 6 months.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet confirms category 3 is probably benign with 6-month LDCT follow-up.

pairs.claim: Pure ground-glass nodules smaller than 6 mm never progress to invasive adenocarcinoma.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: a single pure ground-glass nodule <6 mm does not require routine follow-up in most patients.
pairs.claude_rationale: The snippet is a follow-up policy statement; it does not address natural history, so it neither confirms nor refutes 'never progress'.

pairs.claim: A solid pulmonary nodule with a central, laminated, or popcorn pattern of calcification is very likely benign.
pairs.snippet: Benign calcification patterns — diffuse, central, laminated (concentric), and popcorn (chondroid) — strongly suggest a granuloma or hamartoma and generally require no further workup.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet lists exactly these patterns as strongly benign.

pairs.claim: Macroscopic fat within a solid pulmonary nodule indicates malignancy and warrants resection.
pairs.snippet: The presence of macroscopic intranodular fat is a characteristic feature of a pulmonary hamartoma, a benign lesion, and its identification generally obviates further workup.
pairs.claude_rationale: Intranodular fat signals a benign hamartoma, the opposite of the claim.

pairs.claim: Typical perifissural nodules are usually treated as benign intrapulmonary lymph nodes.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: typical perifissural nodules with a smooth, solid, oval or lentiform morphology most often represent intrapulmonary lymph nodes and do not require the same follow-up as other solid nodules.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet identifies typical perifissural nodules as intrapulmonary lymph nodes needing less follow-up.

pairs.claim: For an indeterminate solid nodule, the Herder model should always be used instead of the Brock model.
pairs.snippet: The Herder model extends the Mayo/Brock risk approach by incorporating FDG-PET avidity to refine malignancy probability for indeterminate nodules.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet frames Herder as a PET-based extension of Brock but does not establish that it should 'always' replace it.

pairs.claim: Vitamin D supplementation reduces the size of pulmonary nodules.
pairs.snippet: Lung-RADS v2022 assigns management categories to screen-detected nodules by size and morphology.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet says nothing about vitamin D or any effect on nodule size.

pairs.claim: A solid incidental nodule larger than 8 mm may be evaluated with CT at 3 months, PET/CT, or tissue sampling.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: for a solid nodule >8 mm, consider CT at 3 months, PET/CT, and/or tissue sampling depending on size, morphology, and clinical risk.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet lists exactly these three options for >8 mm solid nodules.

pairs.claim: A solid nodule that doubles in volume every 25 days is characteristic of a slow-growing indolent malignancy.
pairs.snippet: Very short volume doubling times (<20-30 days) typically indicate infection or inflammation rather than malignancy, whereas most lung cancers show doubling times of roughly 100-400 days.
pairs.claude_rationale: A 25-day doubling time points to infection/inflammation, not indolent malignancy.

pairs.claim: A patient with more than ten bilateral pulmonary nodules necessarily has metastatic disease.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: numerous bilateral nodules may reflect prior granulomatous infection, and management depends on the clinical context and the dominant nodule.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet says such nodules are context-dependent and often infectious, so it cannot resolve the categorical 'necessarily metastatic' claim.

pairs.claim: Air bronchograms within a nodule are associated with an increased likelihood of malignancy.
pairs.snippet: Internal features such as air bronchograms and bubble-like lucencies are seen more frequently in malignant nodules, particularly adenocarcinoma.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet ties air bronchograms to higher malignancy likelihood.

pairs.claim: After a negative baseline LDCT, the next lung cancer screening CT should be performed after five years.
pairs.snippet: Lung cancer screening programs use annual intervals (NLST) or increasing 1-2 year intervals (NELSON) after a negative baseline, not five-year intervals.
pairs.claude_rationale: Screening intervals are annual to biennial, contradicting a five-year interval.

pairs.claim: Beta-carotene supplementation lowers pulmonary nodule detection rates on CT.
pairs.snippet: The Brock (PanCan) model estimates malignancy probability from nodule size, type, count, spiculation, and location.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet is about the Brock risk model and says nothing about beta-carotene or detection rates.

pairs.claim: A subsolid nodule should be confirmed as persistent on follow-up imaging before longer-term surveillance, since many are transient.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: many subsolid nodules are transient (infectious or inflammatory); an initial short-term follow-up CT (e.g., 3-6 months) is used to confirm persistence before longer-term surveillance.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet endorses confirming persistence first because many subsolid nodules are transient.

pairs.claim: A persistent part-solid nodule with a 7 mm solid component can be safely discharged with no further imaging.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: a persistent part-solid nodule with a solid component >=6 mm is suspicious for malignancy and warrants further evaluation (PET/CT, biopsy, or resection).
pairs.claude_rationale: A 7 mm solid component is suspicious and needs workup, contradicting discharge.

pairs.claim: A nodule with an FDG-PET SUVmax of 3.0 mandates surgical resection.
pairs.snippet: FDG-PET avidity raises malignancy probability and feeds risk models such as Herder, but management combines PET findings with nodule size, morphology, and overall clinical risk.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet says PET informs a multifactorial decision; it does not establish that SUVmax 3.0 alone mandates resection.

pairs.claim: Non-surgical biopsy is a reasonable option for an indeterminate nodule of intermediate malignancy probability when a diagnosis would change management.
pairs.snippet: Guidelines endorse image-guided or bronchoscopic biopsy for nodules of intermediate malignancy probability when tissue diagnosis is feasible and would alter management.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet endorses biopsy in exactly this intermediate-probability, management-changing setting.

pairs.claim: Eccentric or stippled calcification within a nodule reliably indicates a benign process.
pairs.snippet: Unlike central, laminated, diffuse, or popcorn patterns, eccentric or punctate/stippled calcification does not exclude malignancy and can be seen in cancers.
pairs.claude_rationale: Eccentric/stippled calcification does not exclude malignancy, contradicting 'reliably benign'.

pairs.claim: Drinking green tea daily prevents the recurrence of resected lung nodules.
pairs.snippet: Volume doubling time helps distinguish benign from malignant nodule growth on serial CT.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet is about doubling time and says nothing about green tea or recurrence.

pairs.claim: For a solid nodule smaller than 6 mm in a high-risk patient, optional CT at 12 months may be considered.
pairs.snippet: Fleischner 2017: for a single solid nodule <6 mm in a high-risk patient, no routine follow-up is required, though optional CT at 12 months may be considered with suspicious morphology or risk factors.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet explicitly allows optional 12-month CT for high-risk sub-6 mm solid nodules.

pairs.claim: A solitary 10 mm solid nodule in a patient with a prior extrathoracic cancer necessarily represents a pulmonary metastasis.
pairs.snippet: In a patient with a history of extrathoracic malignancy, a new solid pulmonary nodule may be a metastasis, a primary lung cancer, or a benign lesion; tissue characterization is often required.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet lists metastasis as one of several possibilities requiring tissue diagnosis, so it cannot confirm 'necessarily a metastasis'.

pairs.claim: Lung-RADS category 4X denotes a nodule with additional imaging features that increase suspicion of malignancy.
pairs.snippet: Lung-RADS v2022 category 4X applies to category 3 or 4 nodules with additional features or imaging findings that increase the suspicion of malignancy.
pairs.claude_rationale: Snippet defines 4X exactly as additional suspicious features on a 3/4 nodule.

pairs.claim: Low-dose CT lung screening produces essentially no false-positive results.
pairs.snippet: A large share of positive LDCT screens are false positives; NLST reported that about 96% of positive results were false positives, mostly benign nodules.
pairs.claude_rationale: NLST's ~96% false-positive rate directly contradicts 'essentially no false positives'.
